title: Extremely slow scaling of minimal Hamming distance in quantum sampling data
authors: P. S. Golubev, I. A. Iakovlev, V. V. Mazurenko
abstract: Quantum data can be obtained from a diverse range of sources, including direct measurements from noisy quantum processors, cold-atom simulators, and classical approximations such as variational neural-network states. However, our ability to characterize these systems is fundamentally limited, as the available measurement data is often sparse compared to the exponentially large Hilbert space of the system. To address this, we propose using the average minimal Hamming distance calculated for a set of unique bitstrings as a robust metric revealing a universal power-law behaviour. Through various examples of real experiments and simulations, we show that the power-law parameters reliably capture the complexity of quantum states and identify quantum phase transitions from limited quantum information, without the need for accumulating extensive statistics or explicitly calculating physical observables. This enables the analysis of completely different quantum experiments within a single framework.
summary: The paper introduces the average minimal Hamming distance as a universal metric to characterize quantum states from sparse measurement data. By fitting this distance to a power law, the authors demonstrate that the resulting parameters can reliably quantify state complexity and detect quantum phase transitions across diverse physical models, including those studied on quantum annealers.
main_problem: The core challenge is characterizing complex quantum states using only sparse measurement data, which is limited by the exponentially large Hilbert space.
main_result: The average minimal Hamming distance ($ ar{r}_1(N_b)$) exhibits a universal power-law scaling, allowing the reliable extraction of quantum state complexity and the identification of phase transitions.
method: The authors propose using the power-law fitting of $ ar{r}_1(N_b) \propto N_b^{-\alpha}$ as a robust metric, comparing results from real quantum hardware, classical simulations, and advanced neural network representations.
model_or_system: The analysis is applied to various quantum systems, including Dicke states, the $J_1-J_2$ model on a square lattice, and spin-glass systems, often simulated using Variational Neural-Network Quantum States (ViT).
key_observables: Average minimal Hamming distance ($ ar{r}_1(N_b)$), power-law parameters ($A$ and $\alpha$), and Intrinsic Dimension (ID).
important_parameters: The exponent $\alpha$ is shown to be extremely slow, and the analysis is sensitive to critical points in the $J_1-J_2$ phase diagram.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the underlying quantum state structure dictates a universal power-law scaling in the minimal Hamming distance, even with limited sampling.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the power-law scaling of $ ar{r}_1(N_b)$ for various states (Dicke, $J_1-J_2$), showing the dependence on system size and phase, and comparing results from different computational methods.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the metric, applies it to diverse physical models (Dicke, $J_1-J_2$), compares results across different computational techniques (ED, ViT, QMC), and concludes by establishing the universality of the power-law scaling for state characterization.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful, data-driven diagnostic tool that bypasses the need to calculate explicit physical observables, making it highly relevant for analyzing noisy, limited-data measurements from NISQ quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Minimal Hamming distance functions calculated for a few notable quantum states (from top to bottom): the classically-sampled 512-qubit Dicke state with 256 excita- tions; 324-qubit spin-glass states simulated on the D-Wave quantum annealer [20] with the square lattice topology and quench times of 7 (the upper yellow curve) and 20 (the lower yellow curve) ns; ground state of the frustrated antiferromag- netic J1 −J2 model optimized with transformer-based neural network quantum state approach [21] on 10 × 10 square lat- tice at J2 = 0.5; 53-qubit Haar-random state imitated on Sycamore quantum processor [22].

FIG. 2. (a) Average minimal distance function, Eq.1 calcu- lated for Dicke states of 24-, 64- and 128-qubit systems in the σz basis. Each point was averaged over 10 samples. Black dashed lines denote the fit of the sampling data with Eq.2. (b) Map distinguishing the different Dicke states with respect to the power-law parameters A and α.

FIG. 3. (a) Power-law parameter, α calculated for exact ED and approximated ViT ground states of the antiferromagnetic J1 −J2 model. Black, blue and green circles correspond to the ViT results obtained for 6 × 6, 8 × 8 and 10 × 10 super- cells, respectively. Red crosses stand for ED solutions of the 6 × 6 system. Red dotted lines denote ED data rescaled as described in the text. The violet plus sign corresponds to the estimate based on pre-trained ViT data from [21]. (b) V-score metric calculated for the ViT solutions with Eq.5.

FIG. 4. (a) Dependence of the power α on the annealing time calculated for D-Wave sampling data [20]. Numbers on the right indicate linear system sizes. These results were averaged over 20 instances. (b) Intrinsic dimension averaged over 20 instances. Red crosses denote the power α (a) and ID (b) calculated for MPS 8 × 8 samples from Ref.20.

FIG. 6. Example of cumulative distributions against µ ob- tained for one specific disorder realization for the 18x18 D- Wave data at ta = 7 ns and ta = 20 ns. The corresponding ID values defined from fit are equal to 21 and 12.59, respec- tively.
